title: Docker Basics: Concepts, Configuration, and First Workloads
excerpt: Learn how Docker works under the hood, how containers are built and run, and how to create your first production-ready workloads using Dockerfiles.

body.value:
Docker containers are lightweight, portable environments that allow applications to run consistently across different systems. Unlike traditional virtual machines, containers share the host operating system kernel, making them faster and more efficient.

Because containers are defined using text-based configuration (like Dockerfiles), they can be versioned, automated, and reproduced with high reliability.

value.title: Key Idea
value.text: Containers are not full virtual machines — they are isolated processes sharing the same OS kernel.

body.value: With a single Docker command, you can launch a fully working web server. Docker will automatically download the required image, create a container, and expose it to the outside world.

This command:

  • Runs a container in detached mode
  • Maps port 8080 (host) to 80 (container)
  • Uses a prebuilt Apache image

Once started, the web server is immediately accessible via http://localhost:8080.

value.caption: Docker Run Flow
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[Run command] --> B[Check local image]
 B -->|Not found| C[Pull from Docker Hub]
 C --> D[Create container]
 D --> E[Start process]
 E --> F[Expose ports]

body.value: Docker is more than just a CLI tool — it is a complete platform consisting of multiple components working together.

value.caption: Docker Architecture
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 CLI[Docker CLI] --> API[REST API]
 API --> Daemon[Docker Daemon]
 Daemon --> Runtime[Container Runtime]
 Runtime --> Containers[Running Containers]
 Daemon --> Images[Docker Images]
 Images --> Registry[Docker Registry]

body.value:
Key components:

  • Docker Engine – the core platform
  • Docker Daemon – manages containers
  • CLI – user interface
  • Images – blueprints for containers
  • Containers – running instances
  • Registry – stores images (e.g. Docker Hub)

value.title: Important
value.text: Docker relies on the Linux kernel. On Windows/macOS, it runs inside a lightweight Linux VM.

body.value: A Docker image is a read-only template. A container is a running instance of that image.

value.caption: Image to Container Lifecycle
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
 Dockerfile --> Image
 Image --> Container
 Container -->|run| Process

value.caption: Example Dockerfile
value.code:
FROM ubuntu:24.04

ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y apache2

COPY index.html /var/www/html/index.html

EXPOSE 80

CMD ["apache2ctl", "-D", "FOREGROUND"]

body.value:
This Dockerfile:

  • Uses Ubuntu as base image
  • Installs Apache
  • Copies custom HTML
  • Exposes port 80
  • Starts the web server

value.caption: Build and run the image
value.code:
docker buildx build -t my-server .
docker run -d -p 8080:80 my-server

body.value: The Docker daemon is the core service that manages containers. Its behavior is configured using the daemon.json file.

value.caption: Useful commands
value.code:
docker ps
docker ps -a
docker stop <container>
docker logs <container>
docker info

body.value: As applications grow, multiple containers must work together. This is where orchestration tools come in.

value.caption: Container Orchestration Options
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
 A[Single Container] --> B[Docker Compose]
 B --> C[Docker Swarm]
 C --> D[Kubernetes]

body.value:
• Docker Compose – multi-container apps on one host
  • Docker Swarm – clustering across hosts
  • Kubernetes – enterprise-grade orchestration

value.title: Final Thought
value.text: If you can define your infrastructure as code, you can automate everything.
